title: 荣誉附件正文｜LOA Combine（第5部分）

To address the above issues, some prior work propose to updating the buffer merely based on the current batch of data with coreset selection mechanisms (Li, Shen, and Chen 2022). Instead, we propose to select representative samples that can best preserve the past data distribution with reference to the states. This is because separating the data selection and buffer update process leads to potential suboptimal model update results. Depending on the state implementation, there are multiple ways to update the buffer, such as generating the reply buffer from a reply- neural-network. Because anti-domain and clustering are in the replay of the data content itself. The buffer content should be selected based on the data maintained by state and the current coreset used for training, as well as the last buffer content, noted as Specifically, we will find some similar sentences/documents from based on expression habits, while being as different as possible from . Stage 4: Model update. In this stage, the framework updates the PLM with the selected coreset and the replay buffer. Specifically, it loads a mini-batch from the coreset and another mini-batch from the buffer , and then updates the model via gradient descent. The model training process is embarrassingly parallel, and thus it is natural to leverage modern parallel hardware to accelerating the model training process. Many existing frameworks such as Pytorch and TensorFlow can be applied. However, it is still challenging because data like sentences have variable lengths, and PLM’s computation patterns are complex. • Challenges, risks & proposed mitigation plans In practice, we expect the PLMs can be updated efficiently to react to evolving data in time. This requires PLMs to continually integrate the information from the recent A-4 RTDS S 22 14 05 1 data to comprehensively grasp the versatile structural and semantic knowledge, such that PLMs could utilize the proper knowledge to boost performance in current downstream tasks. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has achieved our goal. Closely related to our work, several prior studies (Jin et al. 2022; Qin et al. 2022) have proposed methods to progressively expand or retrain PLMs to acquire new knowledge from data streams and mitigate the cataphoric forgetting problem. However, they have mostly used prepared corpora with clearly separated domains, trained in sequence. In contrast, the real data stream is often a mixture of knowledge from different domains. For example, scientific papers generated by arXiv every day can be a mixture of various topics. Those works focus on continual learning in areas of expertise that are clearly spaced out, rather than our focused more realistic settings that different areas of expertise may appear in the same data batch. The importance of learning from boundary-less data streams has been appreciated by many applications recently (Li, Shen, and Chen 2022; Lin et al. 2022), but they are not targeting at PLM maintenance. In particular, they still require labelled datasets during model fine-tuning, which is inapplicable to our targeting problem context.
